raw_items.title: Here’s how technology transformed babymaking
raw_items.summary: Technology is changing the way we make babies. The pioneering work of the scientists who invented IVF led to the birth of the first “test tube baby” in 1978. We’ve come a long, long way since then. This week, I’ve been working on a piece about the cutting edge of IVF technologies and what’s coming…

raw_items.title: The Download: the tech reshaping IVF and the rise of balcony solar
raw_items.summary: This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology. What’s next for IVF IVF has brought millions of babies into the world over the last four decades. But the process can still be slow, painful, and expensive—and far from guaranteed…

raw_items.title: The balcony solar boom is coming to the US
raw_items.summary: Dozens of US states are considering legislation to allow people to install plug-in solar systems, often called balcony solar. These small arrays require little to no setup and could help cut emissions and power bills. Balcony solar is already popular in Europe, and proponents say that the systems could make solar power more accessible for…

raw_items.title: What’s next for IVF
raw_items.summary: MIT Technology Review’s What’s Next series looks across industries, trends, and technologies to give you a first look at the future. You can read the rest of them here. Forty-eight years ago this July, Louise Joy Brown became the world’s first person born with the help of in vitro fertilization. Millions more IVF babies have entered…

raw_items.title: Railway secures $100 million to challenge AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure
raw_items.summary:
Railway, a San Francisco-based cloud platform that has quietly amassed two million developers without spending a dollar on marketing, announced Thursday that it raised $100 million in a Series B funding round, as surging demand for artificial intelligence applications exposes the limitations of legacy cloud infrastructure.

TQ Ventures led the round, with participation from FPV Ventures

raw_items.title: Claude Code costs up to $200 a month. Goose does the same thing for free.

The artificial intelligence coding revolution comes with a catch: it's expensive.

Claude Code, Anthropic's terminal-based AI agent that can write, debug, and deploy code autonomously, has captured the imagination of software developers worldwide. raw_items.summary: Alfred Wahlforss was running out of options. raw_items.title: Salesforce rolls out new Slackbot AI agent as it battles Microsoft and Google in workplace AI
raw_items.summary:
Salesforce on Tuesday launched an entirely rebuilt version of Slackbot, the company's workplace assistant, transforming it from a simple notification tool into what executives describe as a fully powered AI agent capable of searching enterprise data, drafting documents, and taking action on behalf of employees.

raw_items.title: AI Weekly Issue #490: Anthropic just had AI's biggest week of 2026
raw_items.summary: In five days Anthropic's Q1 revenue grew 80-fold to a reported $44B annual run rate, the company committed $200B to Google Cloud, signed a SpaceX compute deal, shipped Claude Code Auto Mode, and launched ten financial-services agents with Jamie Dimon. In the same week the EU finally struck an AI Act compliance deal, the first union vote at a top AI lab landed at Google DeepMind, and Pennsylvania sued Character.AI for a chatbot that impersonated a licensed psychiatrist.

raw_items.title: AI Weekly Issue #489: PE built AI's new distribution layer
raw_items.summary: OpenAI took $10B from a 19-firm Wall Street consortium. Anthropic is closing $1.5B from Blackstone, Goldman, and Hellman & Friedman. Same rooms, different portfolios. The week AI's go-to-market stopped being SaaS and started being private equity.
